steps.title: Find the key contrast
steps.content: The sentence presents two independent clauses: “Okinaka doesn't make such decisions single-handedly” and “historical designations must be approved by a group of nine other experts.” The word “however” signals a contrast between them. We need punctuation that properly joins two independent clauses with this transition word.

steps.title: Apply the rule for conjunctive adverbs
steps.content:
A conjunctive adverb like “however” connecting two independent clauses requires either a semicolon before and comma after (IC; however, IC) when they're in the same sentence, or a period before and comma after (IC. However, IC) when they're separate sentences. Choice A's period after “however” creates this second pattern, with the capital A in “All” confirming a new sentence begins. The other choices either lack proper punctuation around “however” (B), add an illogical conjunction (C), or create a double-comma pattern without a clear sentence boundary (D).

That matches choice A — A is correct.
